19.reason: [Using fscanf with %s reads whitespace-delimited tokens, which includes punctuation attached to words (e.g., "life."). The problem requires words to consist ONLY of letters. This prevents correct parsing and lowercasing of words mixed with punctuation.]

The problem requires words to consist ONLY of letters. This prevents correct parsing and lowercasing of words mixed with punctuation.]

22.reason: [This consumes one character after the word read by fscanf. Since fscanf already stops at whitespace, this likely consumes the first character of the next word or a newline, causing data loss and misalignment.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This consumes one character after the word read by fscanf. Since fscanf already stops at whitespace, this likely consumes the first character of the next word or a newline, causing data loss and misalignment.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  This consumes the character immediately following the word read by fscanf. If it's a letter (part of the next word or same word if delimiters are weird), it is lost. More importantly, fscanf with %s skips leading whitespace and reads until whitespace. The subsequent fgetc consumes the delimiter (space, newline, etc.). However, if the file ends without a newline or has specific formatting, this might skip valid characters or cause issues. But the bigger issue is how words are parsed. fscanf("%s") reads "life." as one token. The code then tries to strip non-alpha chars.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This consumes an extra character after each word read by fscanf, which may skip part of the next word or cause misalignment in reading. Also, fscanf with %s stops at whitespace but doesn't handle punctuation attached to words correctly per problem definition (e.g., "life." should become "life").]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.25 , reason:  [This consumes an extra character after each word, which may skip part of the next word or cause misalignment; also, using fscanf with %s does not properly handle non-alphabetic characters within tokens as per problem definition.]

45.reason: [Loop condition assumes W[i+1].n != 0 as termination, but array may have uninitialized or zeroed entries beyond actual data, leading to premature exit or out-of-bound access. Also, bubble sort starts from index 1, skipping W[0], but data is stored starting from W[1], so this might be okay if intentional—but logic is fragile.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  The loop condition W[i+1].n!=0 is unsafe. It assumes that empty slots have n=0 (which is true initially) but relies on contiguous filled slots. If j exceeds bounds or logic fails, this could access uninitialized memory or miss sorting. Also, bubble sort implementation is inefficient but logically okay if bounds are correct. However, the upper bound isn't checked against array size.

58.reason: This loop does not check if W[j].n is 0 or if j goes out of bounds. If there are no more matches, strcmp will compare against empty strings or garbage if n=0 entries aren't strictly empty strings (they are initialized to "\0" so strcmp returns 0 only if tem is also empty, which it isn't). Wait, if W[j].w is "\0", strcmp("word", "") is not 0. So it stops. But it doesn't check array bounds.
